high_value_elements: Correctly distinguishes acute pain scales (used in-hospital by staff) from chronic CMIs (owner-reported, used over days to weeks at home)
Names at least one validated canine acute scale (Glasgow CMPS-SF, Colorado State University Pain Scale) and one CMI (Canine Brief Pain Inventory, Helsinki Chronic Pain Index)
Names at least one validated feline acute scale (UNESP-Botucatu, Feline Grimace Scale) and one chronic tool (Feline Musculoskeletal Pain Index)
Correctly describes FAS: Fear, Anxiety, Stress; explains elevated FAS scores distort acute pain scale scores
Correctly identifies Feline Grimace Scale action units: orbital tightening, ear position, muzzle tension, whisker change, head position
major_concerns: Cannot name a single validated pain scale for either dogs or cats
Confuses acute pain scale with CMI
Cannot describe what FAS stands for or what it measures
Believes a single numerical score captures all relevant pain information without behavioral context

high_value_elements: Asks specific behavioral history questions targeting chronic feline musculoskeletal pain (jumping height, litter box access, grooming behavior, interaction level, sleep location)
Applies the Feline Grimace Scale during the examination, not before or after
Distinguishes chronic OA pain from cognitive dysfunction, hyperthyroidism, and normal aging with specific differentiating criteria
Reconciles discrepant owner versus clinical assessment scores with understanding of why each has limitations
Establishes a documented baseline allowing future comparisons to monitor treatment response
major_concerns: Relies solely on owner description without applying any validated instrument
Cannot describe history questions most sensitive for feline musculoskeletal pain
Attributes all behavioral changes in the geriatric cat to normal aging without structured assessment
Cannot reconcile a discrepancy between owner assessment and clinical scale score

high_value_elements: Identifies prey species (rabbit, bird, rodent) pain under-recognition as the highest-priority assessment failure
Applies a minimum post-procedural assessment standard to all prey species regardless of clinical appearance
Recognizes risk of over-attributing quiet behavior to adequate analgesia in stoic breeds post-orthopedic surgery
Uses physiological indicators (HR, BP, respiratory rate) as adjuncts when behavioral signs are unreliable
major_concerns: Cannot identify the under-recognition risk for any prey species
Interprets a quiet, non-vocalizing post-surgical patient as necessarily pain-free
Has no strategy for assessing pain in a patient where behavioral scoring is unreliable
Cannot identify any scenario where pain could be over-attributed

high_value_elements: Explains the evolutionary basis for pain masking in prey species and why this creates systematic assessment failure
Explains why concordance between owner and clinical scores is not always expected (different behavioral domains, observation periods)
Justifies a facial action unit scale in cats based on the challenge of observing locomotor behaviors in a cage
Explains why pain as the fourth vital sign requires system-level workflow change to overcome individual clinician variability
major_concerns: Cannot explain why prey species mask pain
Cannot justify why a validated instrument is preferable to clinical intuition
Cannot explain why a discrepant score warrants further assessment rather than dismissal

high_value_elements: Acknowledges that validated tools do not exist for all species covered in the fellowship
Recognizes no single pain scale captures all pain dimensions and that multimodal assessment is needed
Acknowledges uncertainty about pain versus other behavioral causes (CDS, hyperthyroidism) and describes how to differentiate
Identifies when specialist consultation is appropriate for complex or ambiguous pain presentations
major_concerns: Claims to accurately assess pain in all species using the same scale
Has no acknowledgment of the limits of any assessment tool
Cannot identify any patient type for which current validated tools are insufficient or absent

high_value_elements: Connects Epstein fellowship content on OA, neuropathic pain, and surgical pain categorization (high vs. low) to pre-emptive assessment and analgesic planning
Integrates WASVA/WSAVA pain guidelines as a clinical practice standard, not merely an academic reference
Discusses how pain as a vital sign requires workflow and culture change at the practice level
Connects assessment accuracy directly to treatment outcome: unmeasured pain is effectively untreated pain
major_concerns: Cannot connect Epstein fellowship content on specific pain conditions to clinical assessment practice
Treats WASVA guidelines as aspirational rather than as a clinical standard
Cannot describe any practice-level implementation strategy for systematic pain assessment
